briefing.incidentSummary:
₹85,000 was transferred from a senior citizen's bank account to an unknown account at 3:14 AM.

The victim never initiated the transfer. Their phone was found at home.

Someone used a cloned SIM card to intercept the OTP.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Trace the SIM cloning operation

› Identify who intercepted the OTP

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Vikram B
suspects.profileNotes: Accessed victim's SIM provisioning record at 3:05 AM — 9 minutes before the fraudulent transfer. Issued a duplicate SIM request without a physical store visit.

suspects.name: Unknown Caller
suspects.profileNotes: Called victim the previous day posing as a bank employee. Collected account number but NOT OTP — the OTP was intercepted separately via SIM clone.

suspects.name: Rahul V
suspects.profileNotes: Visited the evening before. No technical knowledge of SIM provisioning. Alibi confirmed for 3 AM window.

suspects.name: Deepa S
suspects.profileNotes: Store was closed at 3 AM. Her credentials were not used in the SIM duplication — the backend system was accessed directly.

items.label: OTP received on cloned SIM: 847291
items.detail: Internal message from Vikram B to an unknown number at 3:13 AM — one minute before the transfer. He received and forwarded the OTP directly.

items.label: Transfer done. Delete this.
items.detail: Sent at 3:16 AM — 2 minutes after the fraudulent transfer completed. Confirms Vikram knew about and participated in the fraud.

items.label: Sir your account will be blocked. Please confirm your account number.
items.detail: Phishing call transcript from the day before. Collected account number but not the critical OTP — the OTP interception happened separately via SIM clone.

items.label: Did you get a call from someone pretending to be the bank? My neighbour got scammed that way last week.
items.detail: The victim's friend suggested the attack might be a social engineering call from an external fraudster pretending to be a bank employee. This is a common fraud pattern (vishing). However, the OTP was sent to the victim's original SIM which was simultaneously deactivated — ruling out vishing. The attacker had physical control of the cloned SIM, which requires insider telecom access.
items.irrelevantReason: A vishing attack cannot explain SIM deactivation. The simultaneous SIM swap requires insider access to the telecom operator's provisioning system — pointing to Vikram B.

items.label: sim_provisioning_log.txt
items.detail: Backend provisioning log from the telecom operator's SIM management system. At 03:07 AM, a new SIM card was issued for the victim's mobile number +91-98XXXXXXXX. The request came from an internal operator terminal identified as workstation TL-TERM-14, assigned to the Vikram B. The request type was 'Emergency SIM Replacement' — a privileged operation that bypasses the standard 24-hour cooling period. The old SIM was deactivated at 03:07:44 AM. The new SIM was activated at 03:08:02 AM — an 18-second window. The victim's phone showed 'No Service' from 03:07 AM. This is a classic SIM swap attack executed from inside the operator's own provisioning system.

items.label: bank_transfer_record.pdf
items.detail: Bank transaction record for the victim's savings account. At 03:13:52 AM, an OTP was sent to the victim's number — which by this time was ringing on the cloned SIM held by Vikram B. At 03:13:58 AM, the OTP was entered into the bank's mobile app — a 6-second turnaround, impossibly fast for a sleeping victim but consistent with a prepared attacker waiting. At 03:14:11 AM, ₹85,000 was transferred to account ACC-9921-X. The transfer was marked as 'high-value first-time beneficiary' by the bank's fraud engine but was not blocked because the OTP was valid. The bank's session log shows the transfer was initiated from a device with IMEI 35XXXXXXXXX — not matching any device registered to the account holder.

items.label: employee_access_log.txt
items.detail: Employee access log from the telecom operator's HR and shift management system. Vikram B's assigned shift is 09:00 AM – 06:00 PM, Monday to Saturday. The SIM provisioning terminal TL-TERM-14 was accessed at 03:05 AM — 9 hours outside his authorised shift window. The building access card log shows Vikram B badged into the operations floor at 02:58 AM using his employee card. He badged out at 03:19 AM — a 21-minute visit. No maintenance window, no overtime authorisation, and no incident ticket was open that would justify an off-hours visit. The access card and terminal login together place him at the scene at the exact time of the SIM swap.

hiddenItem.label: destination_account_trace.txt
hiddenItem.detail: Bank KYC documents for account ACC-9921-X, the recipient of the ₹85,000 transfer. The account was opened 3 weeks before the fraud at a cooperative bank branch. KYC documents submitted: Aadhaar card and PAN card both bearing the name 'Vikram Bhardwaj' — consistent with Vikram B's legal name. The account was opened with a minimum deposit of ₹1,000. No other deposits were made until the fraud transfer. The account was not linked to any payroll, utility, or recurring payment — its sole purpose appears to be receiving and withdrawing fraud proceeds. ATM withdrawal of ₹85,000 was made at 04:32 AM at a cash machine 2.1 km from the operator's office.

minigame.title: Trace the SIM Swap Terminal
minigame.hint: Find the internal terminal IP used for the unauthorised SIM swap.
hints: The operator's internal network is on 10.44.x.x.

onSuccess.message: BREAKTHROUGH — destination_account_trace.txt confirmed. The fraud account is KYC-linked to Vikram B's Aadhaar. The money trail leads directly to him.

items.label: OTP Authentication Device
items.detail: OTP was authenticated from device IMEI 867530041234567 — not the victim's registered phone (IMEI 356789012345678). Confirms a different device received the OTP.

items.label: Backend Access IP
items.detail: IP 27.56.88.201 accessed the SIM provisioning backend at 3:05 AM. This IP is assigned to a workstation at SimConnect's night shift office — traced to Vikram B's desk.

timeline.title: Phishing Call
timeline.description: Victim receives fake bank call, account number collected

timeline.title: Backend Access
timeline.description: Vikram B logs into SIM provisioning system off-hours

timeline.title: SIM Cloned
timeline.description: Duplicate SIM issued for victim's number

timeline.title: OTP Intercepted
timeline.description: Bank OTP received on cloned SIM by Vikram B

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Vikram B exposed as the SIM fraud perpetrator.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Vikram B.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...₹85,000. Gone. At 3 in the morning.
The victim was asleep. Their phone never moved.
Someone intercepted the signal itself.
aria.exploreFeeds:
SIM logs. Bank records. OTP device traces.
The fraud was surgical. Find the surgeon.
aria.viewEvidence:
Off-hours access. Unauthorized SIM duplication.
This wasn't random.
aria.decryptionHint:
The mule account has a name behind it.
Decode the reference.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
